content.problemStatement:
We will now look at the classic problem of finding the subarray with maximum sum in an array.

Consider an array $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$, where every $A_i$ is an integer, but can be either positive or negative. We want to find a subarray (ie. a contiguous subset of the elements), whose sum is as large as possible.

For example, if the array is $[-1, 3, 7, -4, 5, -7, 6]$, the answer would be $11$, as the subarray $[3, 7, -4, 5]$ has a sum of $11$, and no other subarray has better.

When you start looking at a possible DP solution, the first choice of sub-problem is to select $DP[i]$ to be the answer for the first $i$ elements. We would like to say something along the lines of $DP[i] = max\{DP[i-1], A_i + DP[i-1]\}$, since there are only two options - either $A_i$ is selected or not selected.

But then when we are considering the term $A_i + DP[i-1]$, we might not be forming a subarray anymore. That is, the contiguous criteria might not be satisfied, since the optimal subarray for $DP[i-1]$ might not include $A_{i-1}$.

This is an issue we'll encounter often in DP problems. What we start off with as the obvious choice for sub-problems (and the definition of $DP[i]$), might not always work out. We might realize that we cannot use the subproblems to get the answer for a large problem, since the optimal subarray for a smaller subproblem might not be easily molded to form the optimal solution for the larger subproblem. 

In such cases, we need to go back to the start and rethink our definition of sub-problems and definition of $DP[i]$. In this problem, there is no issue with the definition of the subproblems - prefixes seem to work fine. But it is the DP definition which is too 'loose' for our situation. We need $A_{i-1}$ to be a part of the subproblem - only then can we try to affix $A_i$ to it to get a larger subarray. So what we do? We just make the definition a bit more constrained, and say:

$DP[i]$ is going to define the maximum sum we can get from a subarray of the first $i$ elements, **but** with the extra constraint that the subarray should end at $A_i$. This seems to be a step in the right direction for our DP equation.

But wait - what would the final answer be? It can't be $DP[N]$, as it need not necessarily end with $A_N$. So all we have to do is just take the maximum of all the DP values, since the actual optimal subarray could end at any index. Great, this seems doable.

So now let's get back to the DP equation. What is $DP[i]$ now?

What are the options? $A_i$ has to definitely be selected. We don't have a choice in that anymore. The only choice is in whether or not we include more elements. If we don't include any more, we get just $A_i$. If we do include more elements, it is now just $A_i + DP[i-1]$.

So, $DP[i] = max\{A_i, A_i + DP[i-1]\}$.

And Final Answer = $\max\{DP[1], DP[2], \ldots, DP[N]\}$.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
